title: Choosing & Tailoring the Approach
summary: How to choose and tailor a delivery approach based on context, constraints, uncertainty, and exam cues.

sections.heading: Why approach selection matters on the PMP exam
sections.body:
On the PMP exam, approach selection is rarely tested as a pure definition question. It is usually embedded in a scenario that signals how the work should be organized, governed, and delivered. The best answer aligns the delivery approach to the nature of the work, the level of uncertainty, the degree of stakeholder engagement needed, and the cadence at which value should be delivered.

PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition emphasizes tailoring rather than applying a one-size-fits-all method. The Agile Practice Guide explains that predictive, iterative, incremental, and agile approaches exist on a continuum, and the project team chooses the approach that best fits the context. Exam questions often reward noticing those signals before picking an action.
sections.tip: Before choosing an answer, identify the implied delivery approach. A technically reasonable action can still be wrong if it fits the wrong life cycle.

sections.heading: Predictive, iterative, incremental, and adaptive life cycles
sections.body:
A predictive life cycle is plan-driven. Scope, schedule, and cost are defined early to the extent possible, and work is managed against approved baselines. Predictive approaches fit best when requirements are relatively stable, changes must be tightly controlled, and the environment favors detailed upfront planning. This is common when work is well understood, dependencies are known, and compliance or contractual obligations require formal control.

An iterative life cycle is used when the product is refined through repeated cycles. Early versions may be incomplete, but feedback helps the team progressively improve the solution. Iterative approaches fit when the solution needs learning and refinement, even if the final product vision is generally understood.

An incremental life cycle delivers the product in pieces. Each increment adds usable capability, so stakeholders receive value progressively rather than waiting for one final delivery. Incremental approaches fit when the solution can be divided into meaningful portions and early delivery of partial functionality is beneficial.

An adaptive life cycle, often referred to in PMI language as agile, is change-driven and optimized for high uncertainty and frequent feedback. Detailed requirements are not locked down at the start. Instead, the team collaborates closely with stakeholders, reprioritizes work frequently, and delivers value in short cycles. The Agile Practice Guide describes adaptive approaches as especially useful when requirements are evolving, technology or customer needs are uncertain, and fast feedback improves outcomes.
sections.tip: Think of predictive as plan first, adaptive as learn fast, iterative as refine repeatedly, and incremental as deliver in slices.

sections.heading: When each approach fits
sections.body:
Predictive fits when requirements are clear and stable, scope can be defined upfront, and formal governance matters. It is often the strongest fit for construction, regulated implementations, fixed-scope contracts, and environments where rework is costly and approvals are formal.

Iterative fits when stakeholders need to see evolving versions before they can confirm what they want. The team expects to revisit and improve the solution through cycles of feedback and refinement.

Incremental fits when the product can be broken into deployable pieces and stakeholders can benefit from partial delivery. It helps organizations realize value sooner while continuing to build out the full solution.

Adaptive or agile fits when uncertainty is high, customer needs may change, feedback is essential, and the team can collaborate frequently with stakeholders. It is useful when value comes from quick learning, rapid prioritization, and continuous adaptation rather than strict adherence to an early plan.

Many real projects combine these patterns. The exam may describe a situation where part of the work is well defined and another part is emerging. That usually points toward hybrid.
sections.tip: If the scenario emphasizes volatility, feedback, and reprioritization, lean agile. If it emphasizes approvals, baselines, and formal change control, lean predictive.

sections.heading: What hybrid means
sections.body:
Hybrid means combining elements from predictive and adaptive approaches in a deliberate way. It is not simply using many tools at once. In PMI terminology, hybrid tailoring selects different methods for different parts of the project, product, or organization based on context.

For example, governance, procurement, stage approvals, and compliance reporting may remain predictive, while solution development occurs in short iterations with a backlog and regular stakeholder reviews. Another common hybrid pattern is using predictive planning for high-level milestones and funding gates, while a delivery team uses agile methods to build and refine features.

Hybrid is appropriate when some requirements are fixed and others are expected to evolve, when organizational governance is formal but the product domain benefits from rapid feedback, or when different workstreams have different levels of uncertainty.
sections.tip: Hybrid is the likely answer when the scenario mixes formal oversight with iterative delivery language.

sections.heading: What tailoring is
sections.body:
Tailoring is the deliberate adaptation of the approach, governance, and artifacts to fit the project's environment, complexity, and objectives. PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition presents tailoring as a core concept: teams should adjust how they work rather than apply every process, document, or control the same way on every effort.

Tailoring can affect process choices, such as how much planning is done upfront, how changes are approved, how risks are reviewed, and how decisions are escalated. It can affect governance, such as the frequency of reviews, approval thresholds, reporting expectations, and compliance checkpoints. It can also affect artifacts, such as how detailed the schedule is, whether a WBS or backlog is the primary scope artifact, and how lightweight or formal documentation should be.

Good tailoring preserves what is necessary for value delivery, compliance, and control while avoiding unnecessary overhead. On the exam, tailoring is usually the PMI-aligned choice when a scenario asks how to adapt the way of working to the project's context.
sections.tip: Tailoring does not mean skipping discipline. It means applying the right amount of process, governance, and documentation for the situation.

sections.heading: Factors that drive the choice of approach
sections.body:
Requirements stability is one of the clearest drivers. Stable, well-understood requirements point toward predictive planning. Evolving or uncertain requirements point toward adaptive or hybrid approaches.

Risk and uncertainty also matter. When technical uncertainty, market uncertainty, or solution ambiguity is high, adaptive methods help the team learn quickly and reduce risk through feedback. When uncertainty is low and the path is clear, predictive methods may be more efficient.

Stakeholder involvement is another major factor. Adaptive approaches depend on frequent stakeholder engagement, quick decisions, and ongoing reprioritization. If stakeholders are available and engaged, agile methods are more viable. If stakeholder interaction is infrequent and requirements must be locked early, predictive methods may fit better.

Regulatory and compliance needs can favor predictive controls, formal documentation, traceability, and approval gates. However, compliance does not automatically rule out agile. Many regulated environments use hybrid approaches, keeping rigorous governance while allowing iterative development within those boundaries.

Delivery cadence is also central. If the organization needs value in frequent small releases, incremental or adaptive approaches fit well. If the product must be delivered as a tightly integrated whole after extensive coordination, predictive delivery may be more suitable.

The Agile Practice Guide and PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition both support choosing an approach based on context rather than ideology. The exam usually rewards that judgment.
sections.tip: Match the method to the work, not to a preference. PMI favors contextual fit over method loyalty.

sections.heading: Predictive exam cues
sections.body:
Predictive questions often include language such as baseline, scope baseline, schedule baseline, cost baseline, work breakdown structure, detailed requirements, formal sign-off, integrated change control, change control board, approved project management plan, and variance against plan.

These cues suggest that planning detail was established early and that changes should be evaluated through formal mechanisms. When you see a WBS, formal baselines, or a change control board, the exam usually expects you to think in predictive terms. Actions such as assessing impact, raising a change request, following the defined change process, and managing against approved plans are often the best fit.

Predictive questions may also emphasize contractual commitments, strict sequencing, fixed deliverables, regulatory checkpoints, or heavy documentation. Those are all clues that plan-driven control matters.
sections.tip: Baseline plus change control board is a strong signal that the answer should respect formal predictive governance.

sections.heading: Agile exam cues
sections.body:
Agile questions often include terms such as sprint, iteration, product backlog, backlog refinement, Product Owner, Scrum Master, daily standup, review, retrospective, user story, velocity, definition of done, servant leadership, and self-managing team.

These cues suggest an adaptive environment where priorities can change and where collaboration and feedback are central. In these scenarios, strong answers often involve working with the Product Owner, refining the backlog, clarifying acceptance criteria, removing impediments, facilitating team collaboration, and incorporating stakeholder feedback in upcoming iterations.

The Agile Practice Guide reinforces that agile responses should favor transparency, inspection, adaptation, and team empowerment over command-and-control behavior. On the exam, avoid forcing predictive mechanisms into a clearly agile scenario unless governance or compliance language specifically requires it.
sections.tip: If the scenario says sprint, backlog, or Product Owner, do not jump to change control board unless the question clearly introduces hybrid governance.

sections.heading: Hybrid exam cues
sections.body:
Hybrid questions often blend language from both worlds. You may see a project with stage gates, formal reporting, and fixed milestone commitments, while the team develops features in iterations. You may see part of the scope fixed by contract and another part managed through a backlog. You may also see governance roles from a predictive environment combined with agile delivery ceremonies.

In these scenarios, the best answer usually acknowledges both sides. It may preserve formal approvals, compliance checks, or contractual boundaries while still supporting iterative delivery, stakeholder reviews, and backlog-based prioritization where appropriate.

Hybrid answers are often stronger when they avoid extremes. A fully predictive response may ignore the need for learning and adaptation, while a purely agile response may ignore governance obligations. The exam often tests whether you can balance both.
sections.tip: When the scenario mixes baselines and backlog language, look for an answer that respects governance while enabling adaptation.

sections.heading: How to match the answer to the implied approach
sections.body:
First, identify the nouns in the scenario. Terms like WBS, baseline, and change request usually point to predictive. Terms like sprint, backlog, and Product Owner usually point to agile. A combination often points to hybrid.

Second, identify what kind of control the scenario expects. Predictive scenarios emphasize plan conformance, approved changes, and variance management. Agile scenarios emphasize feedback, prioritization, collaboration, and adaptation. Hybrid scenarios emphasize both governance discipline and iterative learning.

Third, choose the answer that uses the correct artifact and role for that environment. In predictive, the project manager may review the baseline, consult the risk register, or submit a change request. In agile, the team may refine the backlog with the Product Owner, inspect outcomes in a review, or improve ways of working in a retrospective.

Finally, watch for PMI's broader guidance from PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition. Even when the life cycle differs, good answers still support stewardship, stakeholder engagement, value delivery, quality, and tailoring to context.
sections.tip: Use the scenario vocabulary as evidence. The exam often tells you the method indirectly through its terms.

source: Drawn from the PMP Examination Content Outline, PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, the Agile Practice Guide, and the PMI Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct.
